stem: The nurse is caring for a client with a T6 spinal cord injury who is exhibiting signs of autonomic dysreflexia. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order of implementation, from first to last.

options.text: Elevate the head of the bed to 90 degrees.
Loosen any constrictive clothing or devices.
Assess for bladder distention and catheterize if needed.
Administer nifedipine 10 mg sublingually per PRN order.
Notify the primary healthcare provider of the event.

tabs.title: Clinical Feed (Nurses' Notes)
tabs.content:
Admission Note (Day 1, 08:00):
35-year-old male admitted for management of muscle spasticity related to a T6 spinal cord injury sustained 6 months prior. Client is alert and oriented x4. Intermittent self-catheterization every 6 hours. Reports last bowel movement was two days ago. Baclofen initiated for spasticity. Plan includes physical therapy and bladder training. Client lives alone in a second-floor apartment with no elevator, which presents a barrier to community access; social work consulted. Denies pain at present. Baseline vitals stable.

Progress Note (Day 2, 07:35):
S: Client used call light to report a sudden, severe, pounding headache. States, 'I feel hot and my face is flushed.'
B: 35-year-old male with T6 SCI. History of occasional UTIs. Last bowel movement was 2 days ago. Last catheterization was at 06:00.
A: Vitals at 07:30: BP 210/110 mmHg, HR 50 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air. Client is diaphoretic and flushed above the T6 injury level. Piloerection noted on lower extremities. Suspect autonomic dysreflexia. Head of bed was immediately elevated to 90 degrees.
R: Will assess for and remove noxious stimuli, starting with bladder distention. Will administer PRN nifedipine if SBP remains >180 mmHg after initial interventions. Notifying Dr. Ramirez for further evaluation and orders.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
Time: 07:30 (Focused Assessment)
  • Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Reports severe, pounding headache.
  • Cardiovascular: Bradycardia with HR 50 bpm. S1, S2 audible. No murmurs.
  • Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Unlabored respirations.
  • Integumentary: Diaphoresis noted on forehead, face, and neck (above T6). Skin is flushed and warm to touch above T6. Piloerection (goosebumps) and cool, pale skin noted on bilateral lower extremities (below T6).

tabs.content: Impression: No imaging reports available at this time.

rationale.correct: The correct sequence prioritizes immediate, non-invasive actions to lower the critically high blood pressure and then systematically removes the underlying cause. Autonomic dysreflexia is a medical emergency triggered by a noxious stimulus below the level of a T6 or higher spinal cord injury. The stimulus causes a massive sympathetic response, leading to vasoconstriction and severe hypertension below the injury. The parasympathetic system attempts to compensate by slowing the heart rate (bradycardia) and causing vasodilation above the injury, but this response is inadequate. Elevating the head of the bed is the first priority as it uses gravity (orthostatic hypotension) to help lower blood pressure. Simultaneously removing constrictive items and then addressing the most common trigger (bladder distention) is key to resolving the cause. If these measures fail, pharmacological intervention is necessary, followed by notification of the provider.
rationale.incorrect: Incorrectly sequencing these actions can be life-threatening. Administering medication before attempting to remove the stimulus fails to address the root cause and may lead to rebound hypertension. Delaying head elevation misses the fastest, non-pharmacological method to reduce blood pressure. Notifying the provider before taking any action wastes critical time during a hypertensive crisis where stroke or seizure is imminent. The nurse must act swiftly on established protocols and standing orders to stabilize the client.

answerBreakdown.content: Correct - First Action. Elevating the head of the bed to 90 degrees (high-Fowler's position) is the immediate priority. This action induces orthostatic hypotension, causing blood to pool in the lower extremities and helping to lower cerebral blood pressure, reducing the risk of stroke.

Correct - Second Action. Loosening tight clothing, abdominal binders, or other restrictive devices is a quick, non-invasive way to eliminate a potential source of noxious stimuli that could be triggering or exacerbating the hypertensive crisis.

Correct - Third Action. Bladder distention is the most common cause of autonomic dysreflexia. After initial positioning, the nurse must rapidly assess for and relieve this by checking for kinks in an existing catheter or performing an intermittent catheterization.

Correct - Fourth Action. If the client's systolic blood pressure remains elevated (e.g., >180 mmHg) after initial interventions, administering a rapid-acting antihypertensive like nifedipine is the next step to prevent end-organ damage. This is done per a standing PRN order.

Correct - Fifth Action. After initiating emergency interventions to stabilize the client, the nurse must notify the primary healthcare provider. This communication should include the client's status, the interventions performed, and the client's response to treatment.

clinicalPearls: Autonomic dysreflexia (AD) typically occurs in individuals with spinal cord injuries at or above the T6 level.
The classic triad of symptoms for AD is severe hypertension, pounding headache, and bradycardia. Other signs include flushing/diaphoresis above the injury level and piloerection below.
The most common triggers for AD are related to the bladder (distention, UTI, stones) and bowel (impaction, constipation). Always check bladder first, then bowel.
Never place a client with suspected AD in a supine or Trendelenburg position, as this will worsen the hypertension.
rationale.questionTrap: The primary trap is focusing on pharmacological intervention (giving nifedipine) before addressing the underlying cause. While the blood pressure is dangerously high, the definitive treatment is removing the noxious stimulus. Medication is a supportive measure, not the primary solution.
rationale.mnemonic: Title: 'HIGH & SIT'. Expansion: If the pressure is HIGH, you must SIT them up. This mnemonic reinforces the number one priority action: elevating the head of the bed immediately to lower blood pressure.

pedagogy.nclexCategory: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential
